questions.question:
Comparing the time T1 taken for a single instruction on a pipelined CPU with time T2 taken on a non-pipelined but identical CPU, we can say that

A. T1 <= T2
B. T1 >= T2
C. T1 < T2
D. T1 = T2 plus the time taken for one instruction fetch cycle
questions.to_find: The relationship between execution time for a single instruction on a pipelined CPU (T1) vs non-pipelined CPU (T2)

understand.plain: A pipelined CPU divides instruction execution into multiple stages. For a single instruction, pipelining introduces overhead (pipeline register delays, stage synchronization), so it takes at least as long or longer than a non-pipelined CPU executing the same instruction. Pipelining benefits only appear when many instructions are overlapped.

keywords.explain: A technique where multiple instructions are overlapped in execution by dividing the instruction cycle into stages.
keywords.example: A 5-stage pipeline can have 5 different instructions in different stages simultaneously.

keywords.explain: Extra time added by pipeline registers (latches) between stages that store intermediate results.
keywords.example: Each stage boundary adds a latch delay, so a 5-stage pipeline takes 5 * (stage_time + latch_delay) for one instruction vs just sum_of_stage_times on non-pipelined.

understand.visual_alt: Two timelines: non-pipelined has bare stage times (T2), pipelined for a single instruction has extra latch delays making T1 >= T2.

given.aim: Determine the relationship between T1 (pipelined, one instruction) and T2 (non-pipelined, one instruction) on otherwise identical CPUs.

terms.meaning: Time taken by a pipelined CPU to execute a single instruction end-to-end
terms.example: 5-stage pipeline at 2 ns/stage with 0.2 ns latch: T1 = 5*(2+0.2) = 11 ns
terms.connects: Pipelining adds register/latch overhead to each stage boundary

terms.meaning: Time taken by the equivalent non-pipelined CPU to execute a single instruction
terms.example: Same 5 stages at 2 ns/stage: T2 = 10 ns (no latch overhead)
terms.connects: Without pipelining there is no latch overhead; just the sum of stage execution times

terms.meaning: Registers between pipeline stages that introduce a small delay per stage boundary
terms.example: A 5-stage pipeline has 4 inter-stage latches, each adding propagation delay
terms.connects: These latches make T1 >= T2 for a single instruction

given.plan: For a single instruction, pipelining cannot hide any latency — all stages execute sequentially for that one instruction. Additionally, the inter-stage registers (latches) add overhead. Therefore T1 >= T2.

steps.title: Understand pipelining benefit for a single instruction
steps.apply: For a single instruction, all k pipeline stages are traversed one after another — there is no overlap with other instructions. Each stage also incurs the pipeline register (latch) delay. So T1 = k*(t_stage + t_latch).
steps.note: The throughput advantage of pipelining only appears when multiple instructions are overlapped; for a single instruction, pipelining is actually slower or at best equal.

steps.title: Compare with non-pipelined execution
steps.formula_raw: T_non_pipelined_single = sum of all stage times (no latch overhead)
steps.apply: T2 = k * t_stage (assuming equal stage times). Since t_latch > 0, T1 = k*(t_stage + t_latch) > k*t_stage = T2. In the best theoretical case (t_latch = 0), T1 = T2. Hence T1 >= T2.
steps.note: Option D is a distractor; T1 is not defined as T2 plus one fetch cycle in general. The correct and general relationship is T1 >= T2.

solution.result: T1 >= T2. For a single instruction, the pipelined CPU takes at least as long as the non-pipelined CPU due to pipeline register overhead.

questions.formula_note: Pipeline speedup applies to throughput over many instructions, not to a single instruction's latency. Single-instruction latency: T_pipelined >= T_non_pipelined due to inter-stage latch delays.

questions.question:
An instruction pipeline has five stages where each stage takes 2 nanoseconds and all instructions use all five stages. Branch instructions are not overlapped, i.e., the instruction after the branch is not fetched till the branch instruction is completed. Under ideal conditions:

A. Calculate the average instruction execution time assuming that 20% of all instructions executed are branch instructions.

B. If a branch instruction is a conditional branch instruction, the branch need not be taken. Ignoring the fact that some branch instructions are conditional branch instructions, the following instructions can be overlapped. When 80% of all branch instructions are conditional branches and 50% of the conditional branch instructions are such that the branch is taken, calculate the average instruction execution time.
questions.answer: Part A: 2.8 ns (or equivalently CPI = 1.4 with clock period 2 ns). Part B: 2.4 ns
questions.to_find: Average instruction execution time for a 5-stage, 2 ns/stage pipeline with branch penalties under two scenarios.

understand.plain: A 5-stage pipeline normally completes one instruction every 2 ns (one clock cycle) under ideal conditions. Branch instructions flush the pipeline because the next instruction cannot be fetched until the branch finishes. We need to compute the average time per instruction considering the percentage of branches and how often they are taken.

keywords.explain: Extra clock cycles wasted when a branch instruction disrupts the pipeline because the next instruction is unknown until the branch completes.
keywords.example: In a 5-stage pipeline (2 ns/stage), a branch costs 4 extra cycles = 8 ns penalty, making that instruction take 10 ns total instead of 2 ns.

keywords.explain: Average number of clock cycles needed to complete one instruction.
keywords.example: Ideal CPI = 1; with 20% branches each costing 4 extra cycles, CPI = 1 + 0.2*4 = 1.8.

understand.visual_alt: A 5-stage pipeline diagram showing normal flow (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB) and branch penalty with 4 stall cycles added after the branch instruction.

given.aim: Compute average instruction execution time for a 5-stage, 2 ns/stage pipeline with branch penalties.

terms.meaning: 5 stages, each taking 2 ns; clock cycle = 2 ns
terms.example: One instruction without stalls takes 5 cycles = 10 ns total latency, but throughput is 1 instr / 2 ns (1 per cycle) in steady state
terms.connects: Ideal throughput = 1 cycle = 2 ns per instruction

terms.meaning: Branch instruction must complete all stages before next instruction is fetched: 5 stages - 1 (the branch occupies IF in cycle 1) = 4 extra stall cycles
terms.example: Branch in IF at cycle 1, completes WB at cycle 5; next fetch at cycle 6; penalty = 4 cycles = 8 ns

terms.meaning: Fraction of all instructions that are branches
terms.example: 20% branch fraction means 1 in 5 instructions is a branch
terms.connects: Average extra cycles per instruction = branch_fraction * penalty_cycles

terms.meaning: Among conditional branches, the fraction where the branch is actually taken (pipeline must stall); not-taken branches can be overlapped
terms.example: 50% taken rate among conditional branches means half do not cause stalls
terms.connects: Effective penalty is reduced when some branches are not taken

given.plan: Part A: CPI = 1 (ideal) + branch_fraction * penalty. Part B: Effective branch penalty is applied only to taken conditional branches and unconditional branches; not-taken conditional branches have zero penalty.

steps.title: Part A: Compute average execution time with 20% branches
steps.formula_raw: Avg_time = (1 - f_branch) * t_clock + f_branch * (t_clock + penalty * t_clock)
steps.apply:
Clock period = 2 ns. Branch penalty = 4 cycles (stages 2-5 still need to complete = 4 more cycles after IF). For non-branch (80%): 2 ns each. For branch (20%): 2 + 4*2 = 10 ns each.
Average = 0.8 * 2 + 0.2 * 10 = 1.6 + 2.0 = 3.6 ns.

Alternatively using CPI: CPI = 1 + 0.2 * 4 = 1.8; Avg time = 1.8 * 2 = 3.6 ns.

Note: Some textbooks compute this as effective CPI = 1 for non-branch + 5 cycles for branch (full pipeline drain). If branch takes 5 cycles total: Avg = 0.8*2 + 0.2*(5*2) = 1.6 + 2.0 = 3.6 ns. Still 3.6 ns.

Another common interpretation (used in GATE solutions): since the pipeline stages each take 2 ns and branch stalls mean 4 wasted cycles, avg execution time per instruction = 0.8*2 + 0.2*(2+4*2) = 1.6+2.0 = 3.6 ns.

If the question means instructions complete at rate of 1 every clock in ideal, the average clock cycles per instruction = 1 + 0.2*4 = 1.8 => 1.8*2 = 3.6 ns.
steps.note: The branch penalty is 4 cycles because after fetching the branch in stage 1, we must wait for it to complete all 5 stages before the next instruction can be fetched (stages 2, 3, 4, 5 = 4 more cycles).

steps.title: Part B: Compute average with conditional branches partially overlapped
steps.apply:
Total branches = 20%. Of these, 80% are conditional (= 0.2*0.8 = 16% of all instructions), 20% are unconditional (= 0.2*0.2 = 4% of all instructions).

Unconditional branches: always stall => penalty = 4 cycles. Fraction = 4%.
Conditional branches: stall only when taken (50% of conditional). Taken conditional = 0.16 * 0.50 = 8% of all; Not-taken conditional = 8% (no stall).

Average stall cycles per instruction = 0.04*4 + 0.08*4 + 0.08*0 = 0.16 + 0.32 = 0.48 cycles.
CPI = 1 + 0.48 = 1.48.
Avg time = 1.48 * 2 = 2.96 ns.

Alternative GATE approach treating all branches as 20%, conditional as 80% of those:
Non-branch instructions (80%): 2 ns each.
Unconditional branches (0.2 * 0.2 = 4%): 10 ns each (5 stages * 2 ns).
Conditional taken (0.2 * 0.8 * 0.5 = 8%): 10 ns each.
Conditional not-taken (0.2 * 0.8 * 0.5 = 8%): 2 ns each (overlapped).
Avg = 0.80*2 + 0.04*10 + 0.08*10 + 0.08*2 = 1.6 + 0.4 + 0.8 + 0.16 = 2.96 ns.
steps.note: Key insight: not-taken conditional branches have zero penalty because the next sequential instruction is the correct one; the pipeline can proceed without flushing.

solution.result: Part A: Average instruction execution time = 3.6 ns (CPI = 1.8). Part B: Average instruction execution time = 2.96 ns (CPI = 1.48).

questions.formula_note: Average CPI with branch penalty: CPI = 1 + (branch_fraction * effective_penalty_cycles); multiply by clock period for time. Conditional not-taken branches have zero pipeline penalty.

questions.question:
Consider a 5-stage pipeline: IF (Instruction Fetch), ID (Instruction Decode and register read), EX (Execute), MEM (memory), and WB (Write Back). All (memory or register) reads take place in the second phase of a clock cycle and all writes occur in the first phase. Consider the execution of the following instruction sequence:

I1: sub r2, r1, r4    /* r2 <- r1 - r4 */
I2: add r4, r2, r3    /* r4 <- r2 + r3 */
I3: sw  r3, 100(r1)   /* Mem[r1+100] <- r3 */
I4: sub r3, r4, r2    /* r3 <- r4 - r2 */

A. Show all data dependences between the four instructions.
B. Identify the data hazards.
C. Show that all hazards can be avoided by forwarding in this case.
questions.answer: A. RAW: I2 depends on I1 (r2), I4 depends on I2 (r4), I4 depends on I1 (r2). WAR/WAW: I2 writes r4 which I1 reads (WAR). B. Hazards: I1->I2 on r2 (RAW, 1 cycle gap), I2->I4 on r4 (RAW, 1 cycle gap), I1->I4 on r2 (RAW, 2 cycle gap). C. All RAW hazards can be resolved by forwarding (EX/MEM to EX forwarding paths); no stalls needed.
questions.to_find: Data dependences, data hazards, and whether forwarding eliminates all hazards in the given 5-stage pipeline instruction sequence.

understand.plain: In a pipelined CPU, instructions overlap their execution stages. This creates hazards when one instruction needs a value that a previous instruction has not yet written back. RAW (Read After Write) hazards occur when instruction B needs a register that instruction A is still computing. Forwarding (bypassing) can route the computed value directly from the pipeline register to the next instruction's EX stage, avoiding stalls.

keywords.explain: A later instruction tries to read a register before an earlier instruction has written its result.
keywords.example: I1 writes r2 in WB stage (cycle 5); I2 reads r2 in ID stage (cycle 3) — without forwarding, I2 reads stale r2.

keywords.explain: A hardware mechanism that routes the output of EX or MEM stage directly to the input of the EX stage of a subsequent instruction, avoiding stalls.
keywords.example: I1 completes EX at end of cycle 3; I2 starts EX at cycle 4 — forwarding gives I2 the correct r2 from I1's EX output.

keywords.explain: A later instruction writes a register before an earlier instruction has read it. In standard in-order pipelines, WAR is generally not a structural hazard.
keywords.example: I2 writes r4; I1 reads r4 (I1 reads in ID=cycle 2, I2 writes in WB=cycle 6); since ID comes before WB, no actual WAR hazard.

r2</text><text x="10" y="200" class="sub mut">Forwarding: I1 EX->MEM output forwarded to I2 EX input (r2); I2 EX output forwarded to I4 EX input (r4)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Pipeline execution chart for I1, I2, I4 showing forwarding paths: I1's EX output (r2) forwarded to I2's EX stage, and I2's EX output (r4) forwarded to I4's EX stage.

given.aim: Analyze data dependences, identify hazards, and show forwarding resolves all hazards for the 4-instruction sequence.

terms.term: Reads in second phase, writes in first phase
terms.meaning: Register reads happen in the latter half of the clock cycle; writes happen in the first half. This means WB (write) and ID (read) in the same cycle are safe — the write completes before the read.
terms.example: If I1 is in WB at cycle 5 (writes r2 in first half) and I2 is in ID at cycle 3, no same-cycle conflict. But if both were at cycle 5, the write-first-read-second policy avoids hazard.
terms.connects: This halved-cycle convention means the WB/ID overlap can be handled without forwarding for a 2-cycle gap.

terms.meaning: I1 writes r2; I2 reads r2, writes r4; I3 reads r3, r1 (no writes to registers); I4 reads r4 and r2, writes r3
terms.example: I2 depends on I1's r2 (RAW). I4 depends on I2's r4 (RAW) and I1's r2 (RAW).
terms.connects: These are the source of all RAW hazards in this sequence.

terms.meaning: EX/MEM pipeline register to EX input (EX-EX forwarding) and MEM/WB register to EX input (MEM-EX forwarding)
terms.example: I1 finishes EX at end of cycle 3; I2 starts EX at cycle 4 — EX-EX forwarding delivers r2 to I2 just in time.
terms.connects: Both I1->I2 (1-instruction gap) and I1->I4 (2-instruction gap) and I2->I4 (1-instruction gap) are within forwarding reach.

terms.meaning: Check if any instruction writes a register that a later instruction already read (WAR) or writes the same register (WAW)
terms.example: I1 reads r4; I2 writes r4 — WAR. But in in-order pipeline, I1's ID (read) at cycle 2 is before I2's WB (write) at cycle 6, so no actual WAR hazard.
terms.connects: In standard in-order 5-stage pipelines, WAR and WAW are not structural hazards because reads precede writes temporally.

given.plan: Step 1: List all register dependences (RAW, WAR, WAW). Step 2: Map each to pipeline stages to find actual hazards (where a read occurs before a write has been committed without forwarding). Step 3: Check if EX-EX or MEM-EX forwarding can supply the value in time for each RAW hazard.

steps.title: Part A: Identify all data dependences
steps.formula_raw: RAW: instr j reads register written by instr i (i < j). WAR: instr j writes register read by instr i. WAW: instr j writes register written by instr i.
steps.apply:
I1 writes: r2. I2 writes: r4. I3 writes: memory (no register write). I4 writes: r3.

RAW dependences (producer -> consumer):
- I1 -> I2: I1 writes r2; I2 reads r2. (r2 RAW)
- I2 -> I4: I2 writes r4; I4 reads r4. (r4 RAW)
- I1 -> I4: I1 writes r2; I4 reads r2. (r2 RAW, 2-instruction gap)

WAR dependences (later write, earlier read of same reg):
- I1 reads r4; I2 writes r4 => WAR on r4 (I1 reads r4 in ID at cycle 2, I2 writes r4 in WB at cycle 6; no hazard in in-order pipeline)

WAW dependences:
- None in this sequence.

Summary: 3 RAW dependences, 1 WAR (not a real hazard in-order).
steps.note: In a standard in-order 5-stage pipeline, WAR and WAW are not actual hazards because the later instruction's write cannot overtake the earlier instruction's read/write due to in-order execution.

steps.title: Part B: Identify actual data hazards
steps.formula_raw: Hazard exists when a RAW dependence spans fewer pipeline stages than needed for the value to reach the register file without forwarding.
steps.apply:
Pipeline timing (issue order I1, I2, I3, I4):
- I1: IF(1), ID(2), EX(3), MEM(4), WB(5)
- I2: IF(2), ID(3), EX(4), MEM(5), WB(6)
- I3: IF(3), ID(4), EX(5), MEM(6), WB(7)
- I4: IF(4), ID(5), EX(6), MEM(7), WB(8)

I1->I2 RAW on r2:
  I1 writes r2 in WB at cycle 5 (first half). I2 reads r2 in ID at cycle 3 (second half).
  Cycle 3 read happens BEFORE cycle 5 write => HAZARD. Without forwarding, I2 reads stale r2.

I2->I4 RAW on r4:
  I2 writes r4 in WB at cycle 6 (first half). I4 reads r4 in ID at cycle 5 (second half).
  Cycle 5 read happens BEFORE cycle 6 write => HAZARD. Without forwarding, I4 reads stale r4.

I1->I4 RAW on r2:
  I1 writes r2 in WB at cycle 5 (first half). I4 reads r2 in ID at cycle 5 (second half).
  Cycle 5 write (first half) then cycle 5 read (second half) => NO HAZARD (write-first-read-second in same cycle resolves this).

Summary: 2 actual data hazards: I1->I2 on r2, and I2->I4 on r4.
steps.note: The write-first/read-second convention within a single clock cycle means I1's WB and I4's ID in the same cycle 5 is safe — I1 writes r2 in first half, I4 reads r2 in second half.

steps.title: Part C: Show forwarding resolves all hazards
steps.formula_raw: EX-EX forwarding: value available at end of EX stage, forwarded to next instruction's EX input. MEM-EX forwarding: value from end of MEM stage forwarded to EX input.
steps.apply:
Hazard 1: I1->I2 on r2.
  I1 completes EX (computes r2) at end of cycle 3.
  I2 needs r2 at start of its EX stage = cycle 4.
  EX/MEM pipeline register of I1 at cycle 4 contains r2 => forward from I1's EX/MEM to I2's EX input. Resolved!

Hazard 2: I2->I4 on r4.
  I2 completes EX (computes r4) at end of cycle 4.
  I4 needs r4 at start of its EX stage = cycle 6.
  I2's EX output is in MEM/WB register at cycle 6 => forward from I2's MEM/WB to I4's EX input (MEM-EX forwarding). Resolved!

All RAW hazards are resolved by forwarding. No stall cycles are needed.
I3 has no register output dependences that cause hazards in this sequence.

Conclusion: The pipeline executes all 4 instructions in 8 cycles (no stalls) with forwarding enabled.
steps.note: The key forwarding paths are: (1) EX/MEM-to-EX for a 1-instruction separation (e.g., I1->I2), and (2) MEM/WB-to-EX for a 2-instruction separation (e.g., I2->I4). Since I1->I4 resolves via write-first/read-second, and I1->I2, I2->I4 resolve via forwarding, all hazards are eliminated without stalls.

solution.result: A. RAW dependences: I1->I2 (r2), I2->I4 (r4), I1->I4 (r2). B. Actual hazards: I1->I2 on r2 (I2 reads r2 before I1 writes it), I2->I4 on r4 (I4 reads r4 before I2 writes it). C. Both are resolved by forwarding (EX/MEM->EX for I1->I2; MEM/WB->EX for I2->I4). I1->I4 is resolved by write-first/read-second convention. No stalls required.

questions.formula_note: RAW hazard detection: compare producer's WB cycle with consumer's ID cycle. If ID < WB, a hazard exists. Forwarding: EX/MEM-to-EX covers 1-instruction gap; MEM/WB-to-EX covers 2-instruction gap. Write-first/read-second in same cycle eliminates 3-instruction-gap RAW hazards.
